content.text:
https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/issues/7718 this issue reports that the `posh-git` string now effectively breaks, which is true because we changed something to strip ANSI escape sequences. I want to keep that documentation, which still contains the explanation to use `posh-git` string, but in reality I want people to move towards using the populated JSON (which we do to populate the normal hit segment using the normal template) and not this ANSI-driven environment variable.

We need to update the docs: either remove that part or adjust it so that it talks about how to enable it. Maybe it's already there. I did not check, so validate that, and then we can close this issue with that guideline. Maybe also make an FAQ item on "Hey, this does not render anymore. How do I then migrate from the `posh-git` string environment variable to enabling `posh-git` and leveraging this correctly?"

Base directory for this skill: D:\oh-my-posh\.claude\skills\code-changes

# Code Changes

The workflow for going from an issue, pull request, idea, or feature request to shipped code.
Follow the phases in order. Analysis always comes first; code comes last.

Each phase has a reference file with the full instructions. **Read the reference file when you
enter the phase** — not before, and never skip it because the phase "looks obvious".

## Roles

Assign work based on model capability. The coordinator is not the strongest model — it's the one
that stays resident, owns every phase by default, and knows when it's out of its depth.

| Role        | Capability tier                                               | Owns                                                                          |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| Coordinator | Capable mid-tier, resident for the whole task                | Analysis, plan, delegation, supervision, verification, delivery — by default |
| Escalation  | Strongest reasoning model available; invoked only on trigger | The specific judgment call the coordinator flagged, then control returns     |
| Implementer | Same tier as coordinator, or smaller for trivial edits        | Executing one pinned, self-contained task                                    |

Concrete model names per vendor (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) and how to run the split in Claude
Code, GitHub Copilot, Codex, or IDE agents: [references/model-tiers.md](references/model-tiers.md).

When the current agent already runs at implementer tier, there's no separate delegation step for
standard work: plan and execute directly, still following every phase. Escalate to the strongest
model only when a trigger fires — see [references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — never by
default and never for a routine judgment call the coordinator is equipped to make itself.

## The flow

1. **Analyze** ([references/analyze.md](references/analyze.md)) — root cause and scope,
   validated against the code, never against the report alone.
2. **Plan** ([references/plan.md](references/plan.md)) — pinned spec, task split, parallel vs
   sequential, workspace per task.
3. **Delegate** ([references/delegate.md](references/delegate.md)) — match each task to the
   right executor, coordinator-tier by default.
4. **Supervise** ([references/supervise.md](references/supervise.md)) — monitor, unblock, and
   critically review implementer output.
5. **Verify** ([references/verify.md](references/verify.md)) — quality gates plus functional
   proof, never delegated downward.
6. **Deliver** ([references/deliver.md](references/deliver.md)) — conventional commits and an
   outcome-first report.

Analyze, Supervise, and Verify each carry an escalation checkpoint — see
[references/escalate.md](references/escalate.md) — for handing one specific judgment call to the
strongest available model without giving up ownership of the phase.

**Stop gate:** Phase 1 ends with reporting the analysis and proposed approach to the user and
waiting for a go. Skip the gate only when the user already gave the go in the request itself
("do it", "fix it and commit", "implement with Sonnet").

## Special cases

These entry points replace or extend Phase 1; the rest of the flow applies unchanged.

- Pull request review comments →
  [references/pr-review-comments.md](references/pr-review-comments.md)
- Issue triage → [references/issue-triage.md](references/issue-triage.md)

### What happened?

With edcf3c88f3fb582e84358b385c49d33d04c04224 / #7711 the striping also prevents "normal" ANSI escape sequences to be rendered.

13	## My prompt is broken after upgrading to PowerShell 7.4
14	
15	PowerShell 7.4 has an [issue][pwsh-20733] in encoding which affects the rendering of Oh My Posh.
16	You can work around this by forcing the entire shell to UTF8.
17	
18	```powershell
19	[Console]::OutputEncoding = [Text.Encoding]::UTF8
20	oh-my-posh init pwsh --config ~/custom.omp.json | Invoke-Expression
21	```
22	
23	:::warning
24	Forcing the entire session to UTF8 can have unwanted side effects for other executables. We can't be held liable for that,
25	consider this a temporary workaround until the original PowerShell [issue][pwsh-20733] is resolved.
26	:::
27	
28	## I want to disable the upgrade notice
29	
30	You can disable the upgrade notice using the following command once:
31	
32	```bash
33	oh-my-posh disable notice
34	```
35	
36	If you want to enable it again, run:
37	
38	```bash
39	oh-my-posh enable notice
40	```
41	
42	## The prompt is slow (delay in showing the prompt between commands)
43	
44	:::tip Windows
45	The delay you're experiencing may be linked to the real-time protection features of antivirus software.
46	
47	To potentially alleviate this delay, ensure that Windows Defender or your primary antivirus software has an [exclusion][exclusion] set for the full path of the executable. To identify the full path of the executable in question, you can run the following command in a PowerShell prompt:
48	
49	```powershell
50	(Get-Command oh-my-posh).Source
51	```
52	
53	Additionally, check your antivirus settings for features that actively scan scripts during execution. Even with exclusions set, some antivirus programs might still actively scan scripts, which can introduce a noticeable delay.
54	:::
55	
56	:::caution
57	Always proceed with caution to maintain a balance between system performance and security. Modifying security settings or adding exclusions can expose your system to potential threats.
58	:::

97	## There are rectangles instead of icons in my prompt
98	
99	The font you're using doesn't have the needed standard extended glyph set like [Nerd Font][nf] does.
100	Windows Terminal ships with Cascadia Code by default which has a powerline patched variant called Cascadia Code PL,
101	but also that one misses certain interesting icons. You can fall back to any theme with the `.minimal` indication,
102	or make use of a Nerd Font. Have a look at the [font][font] section for more context in case you're using all the right conditions.
103	
104	## Text decoration styles like bold and dimmed don't work
105	
106	The text decoration styles are based on your terminal emulator's capabilities.
107	
108	A quick way to check if your terminal supports a specific style escape sequence is to take a look at the `terminfo` database, on Linux.
109	Refer [this page on ArchWiki][arch-terminfo].
110	
111	If you are on Windows, use Windows Terminal. It closely copies the `xterm-256color` capabilities.
112	See the [PowerShell docs on terminal support][ps-ansi-docs] and [this GitHub comment][xterm-gh-comment].
113	
114	## Windows Terminal: Unexpected space between segments/text
115	
116	Windows Terminal has some issues with [rendering certain glyphs][wt-glyph]. These issues are on [their backlog][wt-glyphs].
117	A temporary workaround is to use an invisible character at the end (`\u2800`), or a zero width character (`\u200a`) before the icon.
118	
119	In case you didn't export the config yet (it's the default, or you're using the `--config` flag with a predefined theme), you can follow
120	the steps [here][export] to export it to a local file so you can adjust the [segment's][segment] `template`.
121	
122	In the example below, it's assumed that the execution segment's icon `\ueba2` has an unexpected space after it in Windows Terminal.
123	
124	<Config
125	  data={{
126	    type: "executiontime",
127	    template: "\ueba2", // unexpected space
128	  }}
129	/>
130	
131	Adjust it by adding `\u2800` immediately after the icon.
132	
133	<Config
134	  data={{
135	    type: "executiontime",
136	    template: "\ueba2\u2800", // solved
137	  }}
138	/>

150	## Conda: environment name displayed in front of the prompt
151	
152	Conda will automatically prepend the prompt with the name of the environment you're in.
153	To solely rely on Oh My Posh to set the prompt, you can configure the following setting to hide it.
154	Make sure to add this **before** initializing Oh My Posh.
155	
156	```bash
157	conda config --set changeps1 False
158	```

163	To disable this behaviour, set `VIRTUAL_ENV_DISABLE_PROMPT` environment variable to `1` on your system. Example files:

207	## PowerShell: Running in ConstrainedLanguage mode
208	
209	You're here because you've seen the following message:
210	
211	```powershell
212	[WARNING] ConstrainedLanguage mode detected, unable to set console to UTF-8.
213	When using PowerShell in ConstrainedLanguage mode, please set the
214	console mode manually to UTF-8. See here for more information:
215	https://ohmyposh.dev/docs/faq#powershell-running-in-constrainedlanguage-mode
216	```
217	
218	When running PowerShell in ConstrainedLanguage mode, we can't set the console to UTF-8. This will cause the prompt to be rendered incorrectly.
219	There's a few [options][utf-8] to set the console to UTF-8 from an OS perspective on Windows, other systems shouldn't be impacted.
220	
221	To remove the message after manual configuration, you can add the following to your `$PROFILE` before importing Oh My Posh:
222	
223	```powershell
224	$env:POSH_CONSTRAINED_LANGUAGE = 1
225	```

236	The issue is that PowerShell on Windows doesn't yet default to UTF-8. Resolve the issue by setting the shell to UTF-8 in the scope of initializing Oh My Posh.
237	
238	:::info Why it matters?
239	If the location contains non-ASCII characters, non-UTF-8 PowerShell may provide a wrong path to Oh My Posh, which can break the initialization.
240	
241	The scenario for non-ASCII location:
242	
243	- Your computer has a non-ASCII username.
244	- Your [config file](./installation/customize.mdx) is under your `$HOME`.
245	  :::
246	
247	To do so, edit your `$PROFILE`, find the line that initializes Oh My Posh (as highlighted below), and change it to the following:
248	
249	```powershell
250	$previousOutputEncoding = [Console]::OutputEncoding
251	[Console]::OutputEncoding = [Text.Encoding]::UTF8
252	try {
253	    // highlight-start
254	    oh-my-posh init pwsh --config ~/custom.omp.json | Invoke-Expression
255	    // highlight-end
256	} finally {
257	    [Console]::OutputEncoding = $previousOutputEncoding
258	}
259	```
260	
261	Alternatively, let the whole shell become UTF-8. (Be aware: Unwanted side effects can happen.) Add the following line to the top of your `$PROFILE`:
262	
263	```powershell
264	$OutputEncoding = [console]::InputEncoding = [console]::OutputEncoding = New-Object System.Text.UTF8Encoding
265	```
266	
267	## Zsh: No command history (Ctrl+R does not work)
268	
269	This issue occurs when you're using plain zsh in combination with Oh My Posh.
270	You can fix this by adding the right configuration to `~/.zshrc`.
271	
272	```bash
273	HISTFILE=~/.zsh_history
274	HISTSIZE=10000
275	SAVEHIST=10000
276	setopt appendhistory
277	```
278	
279	## ZSH: No such shell function 'zle-line-init'
280	
281	This is most likely caused by two Oh My Posh init lines in your `.zshrc`, remove one of them. See [here][zsh-init].
282	
283	## Repaint the prompt on demand
284	
285	Oh My Posh renders the prompt once per command. When something changes while the prompt is displayed,
286	for example a theme reload or an environment variable set by a script, you can force a re-render
287	by calling a shell-specific function:
288	
289	- Fish: `omp_repaint_prompt`
290	- Zsh: `omp_repaint_prompt`. It's also registered as a zle widget, so it can be bound to a key:
291	
292	  ```zsh
293	  bindkey '^B' omp_repaint_prompt
294	  ```
295	
296	- PowerShell: `Invoke-PoshPromptRepaint`
297	
298	## Fish: Display current bind (Vim) mode
299	
300	By default, Oh My Posh will not re-render the prompt (i.e., generate a new prompt) until a new command is run, so you should
301	export `$fish_bind_mode` to `FISH__BIND_MODE` and call the `omp_repaint_prompt` function to do prompt re-rendering when
302	`$fish_bind_mode` changes:
303	
304	```fish
305	function rerender_on_bind_mode_change --on-variable fish_bind_mode
306	    if test "$fish_bind_mode" != paste -a "$fish_bind_mode" != "$FISH__BIND_MODE"
307	        set -gx FISH__BIND_MODE $fish_bind_mode
308	        omp_repaint_prompt
309	    end
310	end
311	```
312	
313	Next, mask the `fish_default_mode_prompt` function to prevent it from echoing the current mode:
314	
315	```fish
316	function fish_default_mode_prompt --description "Display vi prompt mode"
317	    # This function is masked and does nothing
318	end
319	```
320	
321	After that, you can use the value in a template. The following replicates the [example in the Fish documentation][fish-mode-prompt]:
322	
323	<Config
324	  data={{
325	    type: "text",
326	    template:
327	      '{{ if eq .Env.FISH__BIND_MODE "default" }}<red>[N]</>{{ else if eq .Env.FISH__BIND_MODE "insert" }}<green>[I]</>{{ else if eq .Env.FISH__BIND_MODE "replace_one" }}<green>[R]</>{{ else if eq .Env.FISH__BIND_MODE "visual"}}<brmagenta>[V]</>{{ else }}<red>[?]</>{{ end }}',
328	  }}
329	/>
330	
331	## Fish: Integrate with `Alt+←` and `Alt+→` Bindings to `prevd` and `nextd`
332	
333	Fish supports using `Alt+←` (Alt+Left) and `Alt+→` (Alt+Right) bindings to [navigate the directory history](https://fishshell.com/docs/current/interactive.html#id13).
334	
335	To have oh-my-posh update the directory context as the directory history is navigated, use a function that calls `omp_repaint_prompt` when the `$PWD` variable changes:
336	
337	```fish
338	function rerender_on_dir_change --on-variable PWD
339	  omp_repaint_prompt
340	end
341	```

343	## After updating my Nerd Font to a newer version, the prompt displays unknown characters
344	
345	Nerd Fonts moved the icons to a different location in the font for v3.
346	This can cause the prompt to display unknown characters. There's a built-in migration in Oh My Posh to fix this.
347	
348	To migrate, run the following command:
349	
350	```bash
351	oh-my-posh config migrate glyphs --write
352	```
353	
354	This will update your configuration file to use the new glyph locations. Do know they might look different, as they also
355	updated the icons themselves. A backup of the current config can be found in the same location with a `.bak` extension.

<result>## posh-git default prompt status string — research findings

### Source files (dahlbyk/posh-git, `master` branch — this is still posh-git's actual default branch name, not renamed to `main`)

- **`src/GitPrompt.ps1`** (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dahlbyk/posh-git/master/src/GitPrompt.ps1) — contains `Write-GitStatus`, `Write-GitBranchName`, `Write-GitBranchStatus`, `Write-GitIndexStatus`, `Write-GitWorkingDirStatus`, `Write-GitWorkingDirStatusSummary`, `Write-GitStashCount`, `Write-VcsStatus`, and the `$PoshGitVcsPrompt` script block. There is no separate `GitPromptScriptBlock.ps1` or `WriteGitStatus.ps1` — it's all in this one file.
- **`src/PoshGitTypes.ps1`** (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/dahlbyk/posh-git/master/src/PoshGitTypes.ps1) — defines the `PoshGitPromptSettings` class with all default property values. There is no `GitPromptDefaults.ps1` / `GitPromptSettings.ps1` (both 404) — defaults live directly in this class constructor.
- **`src/GitUtils.ps1`** — confirms untracked files (`??` porcelain status) are folded into `Working.Added`, same bucket as newly-added-but-unstaged files.

### Composition algorithm (`Write-GitStatus`, `GitPrompt.ps1` lines 202–256)

In order, with default settings:

1. `PathStatusSeparator` (default `' '`) — written **before** `[` since `DefaultPromptWriteStatusFirst` defaults to `$false` (prompt path comes first, so a space separates path from status).
2. `BeforeStatus` → `'['` (Yellow)
3. Branch name, **no leading space** (`Write-GitBranchName -NoLeadingSpace`), colored via branch status color.
4. Branch ahead/behind indicator, **with** leading space (`Write-GitBranchStatus`, no `-NoLeadingSpace`).
5. `BeforeIndex` → `''` (empty — contributes nothing by default).
6. If `EnableFileStatus` (default `$true`) and `Status.HasIndex`: staged/index status (`Write-GitIndexStatus`), each field prefixed with a space.
7. If `Status.HasWorking` too: `DelimStatus` → `' |'`.
8. If `EnableFileStatus` and `Status.HasWorking`: unstaged/working status (`Write-GitWorkingDirStatus`), each field space-prefixed.
9. `Write-GitWorkingDirStatusSummary` → one trailing symbol (space-prefixed) summarizing overall dirty state.
10. If `EnableStashStatus` (default **`$false`**) and stash count > 0: stash count wrapped in `BeforeStash`/`AfterStash`.
11. `AfterStatus` → `']'` (Yellow)

### Key default settings (verbatim from `PoshGitTypes.ps1`, `PoshGitPromptSettings` class)

```powershell
[PoshGitCellColor]$BranchColor  = [PoshGitCellColor]::new([ConsoleColor]::Cyan)
[PoshGitCellColor]$IndexColor   = [PoshGitCellColor]::new([ConsoleColor]::DarkGreen)
[PoshGitCellColor]$WorkingColor = [PoshGitCellColor]::new([ConsoleColor]::DarkRed)
[PoshGitCellColor]$StashColor   = [PoshGitCellColor]::new([ConsoleColor]::Red)

[PoshGitTextSpan]$BeforeStatus = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new('[', [ConsoleColor]::Yellow)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$DelimStatus  = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new(' |', [ConsoleColor]::Yellow)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$AfterStatus  = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new(']', [ConsoleColor]::Yellow)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BeforeIndex  = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new('', [ConsoleColor]::DarkGreen)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BeforeStash  = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new(' (', [ConsoleColor]::Red)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$AfterStash   = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new(')', [ConsoleColor]::Red)

[PoshGitTextSpan]$LocalDefaultStatusSymbol = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new('', [ConsoleColor]::DarkGreen)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$LocalWorkingStatusSymbol = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new('!', [ConsoleColor]::DarkRed)
[PoshGitTextSpan]$LocalStagedStatusSymbol  = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new('~', [ConsoleColor]::Cyan)

[PoshGitTextSpan]$BranchGoneStatusSymbol           = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new([char]0x00D7, DarkCyan) # ×
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BranchIdenticalStatusSymbol      = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new([char]0x2261, Cyan)      # ≡
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BranchAheadStatusSymbol          = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new([char]0x2191, Green)     # ↑
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BranchBehindStatusSymbol         = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new([char]0x2193, Red)       # ↓
[PoshGitTextSpan]$BranchBehindAndAheadStatusSymbol = [PoshGitTextSpan]::new([char]0x2195, Yellow)    # ↕

[BranchBehindAndAheadDisplayOptions]$BranchBehindAndAheadDisplay = ::Full   # enum: Full; Compact; Minimal

[string]$FileAddedText       = '+'
[string]$FileModifiedText    = '~'
[string]$FileRemovedText     = '-'
[string]$FileConflictedText  = '!'
[string]$BranchUntrackedText = ''    # nothing shown when no upstream configured

[bool]$EnableStashStatus  = $false   # stash count hidden by default
[bool]$ShowStatusWhenZero = $true    # ← IMPORTANT: +/~/- counters show even when 0, by default
```

Field order within both the index (staged) and working (unstaged) blocks is fixed: **Added(`+`), Modified(`~`), Deleted(`-`), Unmerged(`!`)** — Unmerged is the only one *not* gated by `ShowStatusWhenZero` (it only appears when count > 0).

Ahead/behind rendering (`Write-GitBranchStatus`, `BranchBehindAndAheadDisplay = Full`, the default):
- Ahead only: `"{0}{1}"` → e.g. `↑2`
- Behind only: `"{0}{1}"` → e.g. `↓3`
- Both: `"{0}{1} {2}{3}"` → e.g. `↓3 ↑2`
- No upstream: empty (`BranchUntrackedText = ''`)
- Upstream gone: `×`
- Identical to upstream: `≡`

The overall trailing summary symbol (`Write-GitWorkingDirStatusSummary`) is **not** "before the branch" — it's appended at the very end of the whole status string, after index/working counters: `!` if there are any unstaged (working) changes, else `~` if there are staged-but-uncommitted changes, else nothing (`LocalDefaultStatusSymbol` is empty text). There is no separate "stale/error" symbol before the branch in default settings — a fetch/status error instead replaces the whole bracketed status with `[PoshGitVcsPrompt error: ...]` in `ErrorColor` (Red).

### Worked example for your scenario

Branch `main`, 2 commits ahead of `origin/main`, 0 behind; staged: 2 added + 1 modified; unstaged: 3 modified + 1 untracked (untracked counts as `Working.Added`):

```
[main ↑2 +2 ~1 -0 | +1 ~3 -0 !]
```

Char-by-char build: `[` + `main` + ` ↑2` + (`BeforeIndex` empty) + ` +2 ~1 -0` (index: Added=2, Modified=1, Deleted=0 shown because `ShowStatusWhenZero=$true`; Unmerged=0 omitted) + ` |` (DelimStatus) + ` +1 ~3 -0` (working: Added/untracked=1, Modified=3, Deleted=0; Unmerged omitted) + ` !` (LocalWorkingStatusSymbol, since working changes exist) + `]`.

Colors applied (console-color names, for reference only): `[`/`|`/`]` Yellow, branch name Green (ahead-only uses `BranchAheadStatusSymbol`'s color, which also recolors the whole branch span per `Get-GitBranchStatusColor`), `↑2` Green, staged counters DarkGreen (`IndexColor`), unstaged counters DarkRed (`WorkingColor`), trailing `!` DarkRed (`LocalWorkingStatusSymbol`).

If this repo also had a full path prefix in the default posh-git prompt, a literal space (`PathStatusSeparator`) precedes the `[` — e.g. `C:\repo [main ↑2 +2 ~1 -0 | +1 ~3 -0 !]>`. For an oh-my-posh git-segment template that just reproduces the bracketed status text itself, start at `[`.

### Notes for building the oh-my-posh template

- Every counter (`+N ~N -N`) shows even at zero by default — don't gate on `> 0` unless you intend to deviate from posh-git's actual default (a very common misconception is that it hides zeros; it doesn't, per `ShowStatusWhenZero = $true`).
- Unmerged/conflicted (`!`) is the one exception — only shown when count > 0, in both staged and unstaged sections.
- The separator between the "index" (staged) and "working" (unstaged) groups is `' |'` (space + pipe) and only appears when there ARE working-tree changes (`HasWorking`); if working tree is fully clean, the index group is followed directly by the trailing summary symbol and `]`.
- The trailing single-char summary (`!` / `~` / nothing) is independent of, and additional to, the itemized `+/~/-` counts — it's not a replacement for them.</result>

## posh-git

If you want to display the default [posh-git][poshgit] output, **do not** use this segment
but add the following snippet after initializing Oh My Posh in your `$PROFILE`:

```powershell
function Set-PoshGitStatus {
    $global:GitStatus = Get-GitStatus
    $env:POSH_GIT_STRING = Write-GitStatus -Status $global:GitStatus
}
New-Alias -Name 'Set-PoshContext' -Value 'Set-PoshGitStatus' -Scope Global -Force
```

You can then use the `POSH_GIT_STRING` environment variable in a [text segment][text]:

<Config
  data={{
    template:
      "{{ if .Env.POSH_GIT_STRING }} {{ .Env.POSH_GIT_STRING }} {{ end }}",
  }}
/>
input.new_string:
## posh-git

Use the `source: pwsh` option to fetch status information from the [posh-git][poshgit] PowerShell
module instead of the `git` CLI. This populates the same template properties as above, so you're
not limited to what `Write-GitStatus` prints, and nothing relies on ANSI escape sequences.

<Config
  data={{
    type: "git",
    style: "plain",
    template:
      "[{{ .HEAD }}{{ if .BranchStatus }} {{ .BranchStatus }}{{ end }}{{ if .Staging.Changed }} +{{ .Staging.Added }} ~{{ .Staging.Modified }} -{{ .Staging.Deleted }}{{ if gt .Staging.Unmerged 0 }} !{{ .Staging.Unmerged }}{{ end }}{{ end }}{{ if and .Staging.Changed .Working.Changed }} |{{ end }}{{ if .Working.Changed }} +{{ add .Working.Added .Working.Untracked }} ~{{ .Working.Modified }} -{{ .Working.Deleted }}{{ if gt .Working.Unmerged 0 }} !{{ .Working.Unmerged }}{{ end }}{{ end }}{{ if .Working.Changed }} !{{ else if .Staging.Changed }} ~{{ end }}]",
    options: {
      fetch_status: true,
      source: "pwsh",
    },
  }}
/>

The template above reproduces posh-git's default `[branch ↑ahead ↓behind +added ~modified -deleted | +added ~modified -deleted !]`
format, counting untracked files as added in the working tree, the same way posh-git does. Adjust icons, order,
or gating to taste, it's a regular [template][templates] now.

:::info

Prior to v29.35.1, a `POSH_GIT_STRING` environment variable set by `Write-GitStatus` and consumed through a
[text segment][text] was the documented way to get this output. That variable carries raw ANSI escape
sequences, which Oh My Posh now strips from environment variable values, so the string no longer renders.
`source: pwsh` above is the supported replacement, see the [FAQ][faq-posh-git] for details.

:::

## My posh-git prompt string doesn't render anymore

You're relying on the `POSH_GIT_STRING` environment variable, set via `Write-GitStatus` and shown through a
[text segment][text], and instead of your git status you now see raw escape codes like `[93m[[39m...`.

`POSH_GIT_STRING` carries ANSI escape sequences, and Oh My Posh strips ANSI escape sequences from environment
variable values before rendering them, so the string can no longer display as intended. This isn't a
regression to revert, environment variables aren't meant to carry raw ANSI, and stripping them closes a
class of prompt-injection issues.

Migrate to the git segment's `source: pwsh` option instead. It fetches the same data from the [posh-git][poshgit]
module, but through the segment's regular template properties (`.Staging`, `.Working`, `.BranchStatus`, ...)
rather than a pre-rendered, ANSI-laden string, so nothing gets stripped. See the [posh-git section][git-poshgit]
of the git segment docs for a sample template that reproduces posh-git's default output.
